title: Listening - Part 4: Learning a New Language Late in Life
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people over sixty talking about their experience of learning a new language

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d always been a bit intimidated by languages at school, but [21]when my daughter married a Brazilian, I knew I had to learn Portuguese to talk to my new grandchildren[/21]. It’s been much harder than I expected! My memory isn't what it used to be, and [26]the most embarrassing part is my terrible accent, which often leads to hilarious misunderstandings[/26] during our video calls. I’ve said some very strange things when I meant to ask about their school day! Still, the effort is worth it for that moment when the kids finally understand what I’m saying and their faces light up. || Speaker 2: I’ve always been active, but after I retired, I felt like my brain was starting to get a bit 'lazy'. [22]I took up Japanese primarily because I wanted a mental challenge that was completely different from my old job in engineering[/22]. It’s like learning a secret code. [27]What I find most satisfying is the way it has opened up a whole new world of cinema and literature that I never could have accessed before[/27]. I now spend my evenings watching classic films without the subtitles, and it’s a wonderful feeling of accomplishment. It’s definitely kept me sharp and given me a new hobby for my golden years. || Speaker 3: My late husband and I always talked about moving to Italy, but we never got around to it. [23]I decided to start learning Italian as a way to honor his memory and finally make that trip we’d planned[/23]. It was a very emotional process at first. [28]The biggest hurdle for me has been finding the courage to actually speak to people in public[/28]. I know the grammar, but I freeze up when a waiter asks me a question. I’m slowly getting better, and I’ve realized that most people are actually very patient and encouraging if they see you’re trying. || Speaker 4: I’m a firm believer in lifelong learning. [24]I started learning Mandarin because I was fascinated by the economic rise of China and wanted to understand the culture better[/24]. It’s been a fascinating journey. However, [29]I was surprised by how much the physical act of writing the characters helped my manual dexterity[/29]. I’ve always had a bit of arthritis in my hands, but the precise movements required for calligraphy have actually made them feel more flexible. It’s an unexpected physical benefit to a purely intellectual pursuit. I never thought I’d be able to draw such beautiful characters. || Speaker 5: I’d lived in France for years but always relied on my husband to do the talking. [25]After he passed away, I realized I was isolated and needed to learn the language to properly integrate into my village[/25]. It was a matter of survival, really. [30]The most rewarding aspect has been the wonderful new friendships I’ve made at the local senior center[/30]. Now that I can join in the conversations about the weather or the local news, I finally feel like I belong here. I’m no longer the 'English lady' who just smiles and nods; I’m part of the community, and that’s a wonderful feeling.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) each speaker's main motivation for learning a language.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 wants to communicate with her Brazilian grandchildren.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted a mental challenge to keep his brain sharp after retirement.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 mentions learning Italian to honor her husband's memory and make a trip they'd planned.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 mentions a fascination with China's economic rise and wanting to understand the culture.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 needed to learn the language to integrate and avoid isolation after her husband's death.

options.D: to prepare for a permanent move abroad
options.E: to fulfill a long-standing personal promise
options.H: to become more involved in local life

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker found most challenging or rewarding about the process.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions being embarrassed by her accent and the misunderstandings it causes.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 mentions the satisfaction of accessing cinema and literature in the original language.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 mentions the difficulty and fear of speaking to people in public.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 mentions the unexpected benefit of improved manual dexterity from calligraphy.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 highlights the rewarding aspect of making new friends and feeling a sense of belonging.

options.A: the time required for daily practice
options.B: the discovery of new cultural media
options.D: a lack of confidence in their pronunciation
options.E: the complexity of the grammar rules
options.F: an improvement in a physical condition
options.G: overcoming a fear of making mistakes
options.H: the development of a social network
